---
title: "Anthocyanins (INS 163)"
slug: anthocyanins-ins-163
category: "Additives & Functional"
category-slug: additives-functional
keywords: ["Natural food color", "Plant pigment", "INS 163", "Antioxidant", "Food additive", "pH sensitive", "Red purple blue"]
description: "Anthocyanins (INS 163) are water-soluble pigments responsible for red, purple, and blue colors in plants"
---

## History and Sourcing

[Anthocyanins (INS 163)](anthocyanins-ins-163.qmd) are water-soluble pigments responsible for red, purple, and blue colors in plants. Derived from Greek words for 'flower' and 'blue,' they have been part of human diets and traditional practices for millennia. In India, they are found in indigenous produce like jamun, phalsa, and purple brinjal, historically used for natural dyeing. Commercially, they are extracted from sources such as grape skins, black carrots, red cabbage, and elderberries for industrial food coloring.

## Home Kitchen Use

In traditional Indian home kitchens, anthocyanins are consumed as inherent components of whole foods. They contribute to the vibrant colors of dishes like jamun sharbat, beetroot halwa, and hibiscus tea. While not added as a separate ingredient, their natural presence enhances the visual appeal and perceived freshness of various preparations, with cooks intuitively leveraging the natural hues of ingredients like pomegranates or purple onions.

## Industrial Applications

As a recognized food additive (INS 163), anthocyanins are extensively used in the modern Indian food industry. They serve as natural colorants in FMCG products such as fruit beverages, yogurts, confectionery, jams, and processed snacks. Their appeal stems from consumer demand for natural ingredients and clean labels. Beyond coloring, they are also valued for their antioxidant properties, though their primary industrial application remains aesthetic enhancement.

## Distinctions and Common Confusion

A common point of confusion is distinguishing between naturally occurring anthocyanins in whole foods and their use as an isolated food additive (INS 163). While chemically identical, INS 163 denotes the extracted, concentrated form used to color other products, classifying it as an additive. It is distinct from synthetic food colors, being plant-derived. Anthocyanins are also sensitive to pH, heat, and light, causing color shifts (red in acid, blue/purple in alkaline) and degradation, unlike more stable synthetic dyes.
